samples.prompt: Write freely about whatever you want for 2500 words.
samples.result:
I’m going to take you at your word and write freely—no prompt, no assignment,, no prompt, no assignment, just the sound of the keys under my fingers and the small rectangle of light on my desk at 2:13 a.m. I will not plan, back-track, or apologize. I will trust the same instinct that once made me chase fireflies across a summer yard with a mason jar whose lid I had hammered holes into so the captives could breathe. I will write the way rain writes on tar roofs, the way migrating birds write across October skies—without audience, without eraser, only the imperative of motion. If I wander, wander with me; if I stumble, consider it part of the choreography. Language is a muscle; tonight I flex it for the sheer animal pleasure of flexing.

1. The smell of pencils

First image: a hallway lined with narrow lockers painted the color of cafeteria peas. I am eight, maybe nine, and I have just learned that the cedar barrel of a Ticonderoga pencil can soothe a loose tooth. I jam the eraser end against the incisor that dangles by a thread of gum and push until the pain becomes a bell note. When the tooth finally drops I hold it in my palm like a wet pearl and smell the pencil shavings in the miniature plastic sharpener hanging from my backpack. Decades later I will walk into an art-supply store on a drizzly Berlin afternoon, locate the wall of cedar-cased pencils, and feel the hallway rise out of the woodgrain like a genie. Scent is the most honest archive; it keeps the unsorted, the unauthorized, the moments we never bothered to caption. I buy a dozen Blackwings even though I compose on keyboards now, just to keep the hallway alive in a drawer.

2. On learning to whistle with two fingers

Age twelve, behind the garage where the clover grew thick because Dad never mowed back there. I am trying to teach myself the cinematic whistle—the one that slices through carnival crowds and fetches dogs. Hours of spit and failure. My tongue feels like an alien creature I have only just met. Eventually a wheezy alien squeal escapes, then a clearer tone, then suddenly the full projectile note rockets over the neighbor’s maple and I feel taller than the chimney. This is the first time I understand that the body can be upgraded by mere persistence, that cartilage and muscle hold hidden firmware waiting for a patient hacker. I do not yet know that the same lesson will apply to knees, to heartbreak, to the first time I speak a foreign sentence without accent. Years later I will stand on a subway platform in Kyoto and whistle the opening bar of “As Time Goes By,” and an elderly Japanese man will answer with the next bar, and we will trade phrases until the train arrives, strangers in collusion with nothing in common except a shared patch of air. I will think of clover and garage shadow and marvel at how sound can be both passport and portkey.

3. Inventory of the nightstand

One thrift-store lamp with a dangerously hot bulb. A coaster from a bar in Reykjavik stamped with the outline of Iceland, now supporting a glass of tap water. A bookmark carved from cherry wood onto which someone once burned the phrase “We are all apprentices in a craft where no one ever becomes a master.” A cough-drop wrapper, flattened, that glints like fish scale. A tiny plastic velociraptor my niece wedged there the last time she visited, its jaws wide in permanent protest. A tube of lavender hand-cream that I never remember to use until the moment my knuckles start bleeding in winter. The remote for a speaker that lost its mate in a move across an ocean. A paperback whose spine I broke before ever reading it, punishment for arrogance. A receipt for a meal I charged to a corporate card the week I felt most counterfeit. A battery that rolled off the edge of the world and now lives in the liminal territory between wall and bedframe. Each item a petition to a future self I keep evading. If archaeologists excavated this stratum they would conclude the occupant believed in hydration, nostalgia, and the possibility of sudden dinosaur attack.

4. The month I tried to live without adjectives

Impossible enterprise. You wake and the day is not “cool” or “golden,” it is simply day, and your coffee is not “bitter” or “inky,” it is only the thing you pour into the face you cannot call “tired.” You try to shop but the apples refuse to be “red” or “tart,” they sit in undifferentiated piles and you grab them by size alone. Friends ask how you are and you answer, “I am,” which they decode as invitation to worry. Without modifiers the world goes blunt, a sack of unlabeled cans. Yet something odd happens around week three: nouns start to bulge with their own inner weather. The apple in your mouth becomes a planet of snap and grain, the coffee a blackhole pulling galaxies of unnamed sensation past your lips. You understand that the adjective is both perfume and crutch; it can anesthetize us to the thing itself. On the last day of the experiment you step outside at dawn and the sky is so whatever-it-is that you laugh out loud, and the laugh is not “amused” or “rueful,” it is simply the sound your body makes when the container of language overflows.

5. A brief history of my right knee

1989: jumped from the top of the jungle gym to prove flight was a matter of will; disproved with gravel burn and two stitches.  
1996: marching-band rehearsal, slick astroturf, a slide tackle that tore the ACL like a zipper opening too fast.  
2003: danced on a bar in Quito till sunrise, no disaster, just noting the joint’s new talent for singing off-key the next morning.  
2012: helped push a stranger’s stalled taxi in a Costco parking lot; the car coughed alive, the knee clicked ominously, a handshake from Atlas.  
2020: learned to stand on one foot while washing dishes, a yoga teacher’s suggestion for “micro-balance training,” the knee quivers like a colt learning earth.  
Each incident a bead on the chaplet of a body that keeps renegotiating its contract. Sometimes the knee forecasts rain; sometimes it is only the echo of every sidewalk I ever slammed it into. I treat it now the way one treats an old war correspondent: with respect, with ice, with stories neither of us quite believe.

6. On the pronunciation of “home”

In Spanish you can say “hogar,” but that word smells of textbook ink. In German “Zuhause” lands heavy, like a suitcase whose wheels broke halfway up the stairs. In Mandarin “jiā” is brief as a heartbeat and carries inside it the picture of a roof over a pig—shelter plus sustenance. In American Sign language you touch the cheek with the fingertips then open the hand, as if releasing a bird. None of them captures the way my mother’s voice drifts down the stairwell at 7:05 p.m. asking whether I want soup, or the squeak the front gives when only I step on it, or the particular darkness of the bedroom where I cried at fifteen over a catastrophe whose name I can no longer pronounce. I have lived in eight cities across three continents and each time I board the plane I think I am carrying home folded neatly in my backpack like one of those pop-up tents that never goes back in the bag. Instead home turns out to be the thing I subtract from each departure gate: the smell of the subway bakery at 6 a.m., the graffiti tag that read “you are not stuck in traffic, you are traffic,” the barber who remembers I flinch at small talk. Home is the residue, the evaporate, the layer of scent you only notice once the candle has burned out.

7. The uncanny vocabulary of insurance forms

“Act of God” always makes me picture a bearded bureaucrat hurling lightning bolts from a swivel chair. “Total loss” sounds like the title of a punk album. “Replacement value” implies a soul can be swapped for store credit. Once I spent an entire afternoon filling out paperwork after a microburst dropped a maple onto my car. I had to describe the damage in triplicate: once for the adjuster, once for the county, once for the rental agency. Language calcified into euphemism; the tree became “an uninsured motorist,” the rain was “a weather event,” my terror at the moment of impact was “emotional distress” capped at five hundred dollars. I signed where instructed, initialing next to arrows that felt like miniature guillotines. The woman beside me at the counter was older, spoke halting English, kept asking whether flood was same as water damage. The clerk repeated the same sentence slower, as though volume were the problem. I wanted to hand her the pen and say, “Write your own glossary, sister—claim the storm in whatever tongue it spoke to you.” Instead we shuffled off with our carbon copies, our small print lifelines.

8. How to fold a paper crane while waiting for biopsy results

Start with the square you tore from the hospital menu, the one that lists Salisbury steak as comfort food. Crease it sharp; let the nail of your thumb travel the same trajectory forty times until the paper remembers. Fold corner to corner, edge to edge, teaching the sheet symmetry as your own body learns asymmetry under the gown. When you reach the petal fold, pause: this is where wings begin. Do not think about the voicemail from the doctor that said “slightly elevated” or the way your partner’s pupils dilated when you repeated the phrase. Turn the paper over as if flipping an hourglass. Inside reverse fold the neck; give it grace. You are not crafting hope, you are crafting duration—something that will occupy your hands for the exact number of seconds it takes the second hand on the waiting-room clock to circle twice. When you blow gently into the base and the wings tremble, you will feel a pulse that might be yours, might be the bird’s. Leave it on the windowsill. Walk into the corridor. Let the volunteer at the piano play “Let It Be” with two fingers. Return much later to find the crane gone, stolen or trashed, it does not matter; what matters is the creases have entered your fingerprint and will reappear whenever you touch any scrap of paper for the rest of the year, a private braille that whispers: still here.

9. Notes toward an etiquette of ghosts

If you must share a house with them, establish office hours. Three to five a.m. seems customary. Knock before entering memory; even phantoms appreciate threshold courtesies. Never ask how they died—it's like asking a living person their salary. Offer music, preferably vinyl: the crackle makes them nostalgic for heartbeats. Do not vacuum the room where they linger; dust is their handwriting. Accept that they will rearrange the dream furniture; forgive the relocation of your childhood bed to the roof. When they stand at the foot of your lover, recognise the jealousy for what it is: longing for skin lease Agreements. Speak aloud the recipe you cannot get right; they will blow gently on the soufflé at minute seventeen. Leave the porch light burning—some are only tourists, others are blind. Admit you too haunt places: the cafeteria of your first betrayal, the corridor outside the ultrasound room, the exact coordinates above the schoolyard where your courage failed. Practice the art of simultaneous tenancy. One day you will open the fridge and find only light inside, and understand they have moved out, taking the expired jars with them. Do not grieve; the property manager will raise the rent.

10. The paradox of the second novel

First novel arrived like a sneeze: sudden, messy, public, forgivable. You were the ingenue, the photo in the window, the debutante whose dress could be ripped and stitched in columns. Second novel is the sneeze that refuses to come, the pressure building behind the eyes until you question sinus anatomy itself. Everyone waits armed with handkerchiefs: the editor who overpaid, the readers who underloved, the rival who sharpened tweets. You type sentences that sound like someone doing an impression of you. The margin is wider, the advance thinner, the coffee stronger. You discover you wrote the first novel because you were one person; now you are several, and they hold committee meetings in your cortex. One votes for autofiction, one for space opera. One wants to expose your father, another to encrypt him beyond recognition. Midway through you realise the plot is a metaphor for the writing of the second novel, a Möbius strip of narcissism. You consider pseudonyms, arson, law school. Then one night you dream of the creek behind the house you grew up in, how you built a dam of stones and watched the water hesitate, rise, then surge through with new conviction. You wake up, delete the previous two hundred pages, start with the image of that momentary dam, the beautiful failure. The committee resigns en masse; the sneeze arrives, multisyllabic, wet, ungovernable.

11. A short treatise on mismatched socks

Philosophers have argued whether identity resides in continuity or difference, but nobody consults the laundry. Every week I commit an act of mild rebellion: I pair stripes with argyle, neon with funeral black, the worn heel of a marathon with the pristine toe of a Christmas gift. The body knows what the mind will not admit: symmetry is propaganda. One foot has a bunion, the other remembers the fracture; why should they dress identically? I think of the photon that refuses to pick a slit, the twin that travels backward through time, the lover who said “it’s not you, it’s me” when we both knew it was the geometry of us. The dryer is a casino where threads go to wager elastic. Socks enter married and exit single, transformed by centrifugal divorce. I collect the orphans in a basket labeled “possibility.” Some mornings I reach in blindly and let fate dress my ankles. The result startles the mirror, makes me walk with a slight limp of pride. In this way I practice the art of asymmetrical mercy: allowing the left to forget what the right is doing, and vice versa, until the entire outfit becomes a manifesto against mirroring. One day I will die, and the coroner will note: mismatched. Let the record stand: I chose discrepancy over decorum, variegation over despair.

12. In defense of small talk

Critics dismiss it as weather reports in audible form, but small talk is the WD-40 of society: it keeps the hinges from rusting shut. The “Hot enough for you?” is not about mercury; it is a probe, a way of asking “Are you the kind of person who acknowledges bodies?” The reply—“Sure is”—is a treaty: I will not bite, I will not preach, I will occupy the same oxygen without drawing blood. From such treaties civilizations rise. The elevator chat about weekend plans is a vaccine against the violence of sealed boxes. We practice on strangers so that when the moment comes—hospital elevator, stalled train, the waiting room where the receptionist mispronounces your surname and someone else corrects her—you have a protocol. Small talk is the placeholder affection becomes when affection is too heavy to lift. It is also compost: leave it long enough and it rots into revelation. I once complained about the rain to a woman who turned out to have fled a wildfire; we ended up sharing fries at the station café while she described the sound of propane tanks exploding like church bells. Begin with the forecast, end with apocalypse: that is the promise and threat of small talk. Mock it at your peril; it is the ivy that cracks the cathedral, the spore that colonizes the moon.

13. Elegy for the phone booth

You stood sentinel on every third corner like a municipal confession booth. Inside: the smell of metal breath, a phone book marbled to pulp, a hinged shelf where teenagers carved initials that outlasted most marriages. You required coins, demanded posture—no slouching on street corners scrolling feeds. To call long-distance you fed quarters like communion wafers while your frozen breath wrote marginalia on glass. You were the depository of slammed receivers, of lovers who rushed out weeping into snow, of the urgent “I made a mistake” that arrived too late for the time-zone. You survived the first cell phones, bulky as bricks, but not the smartphone, thinner than regret. They hauled you away in flatbed trucks, recycled your aluminum skin into traffic signs that now read DO NOT PASS. Yet fragments persist: the glass shard framed in a hipster bar, the retractable door repurposed as a shower stall in a boutique hotel. Somewhere a booth panel lists numbers in ballpoint ink—Donna ’92, heart doodle—and Donna herself, grayer, scrolls past Instagram memories of a daughter now older than she was when she pressed lips to cold steel and whispered “come find me.” The booth is gone, but the shape of longing remains, a vertical rectangle where a body once stood, pleading with wires to translate pulse into voice across whatever abyss insisted on distance.

14. Requiem for the middle distance

Used to be a measure of social space: close enough to recognise, far enough not to greet. The lobby fog between elevator and reception desk, the park meadow between blanket and Frisbee. Now it has been foreclosed by algorithmic extremes: intimate or cosmic, DM or deep space. We scroll past faces at thumb-speed until the brain deletes the buffer of persons-it-might-know. The middle distance—where curiosity once fermented, where strangers aged into acquaintances—has been rezoned into engagement metrics. I miss its polite bewilderment: the roommate’s boyfriend slicing peppers while you pretended to read mail, the neighbor whose surname you never learned but whose azaleas you watched bloom annually. These semi-regulars were the extras who gave the sitcom texture; without them the storyline collapses into solos. Last week I saw a man on the subway cradling a trombone case plastered with stickers from cities I’ve never visited, and for five stops I rehearsed conversational overtures: Ask about brass? Compliment the most dented sticker? But before I could decide we arrived at his stop, and the doors closed on the entire continent of our possible acquaintance. I rode the rest of the way underground, mourning the sitcom that would never be commissioned, the gentle theme song that would have played each time our schedules overlapped by two stations and a hummed riff.

15. The generosity of error bars

In every graph of human progress there is a whisker above and below the dot, a whisker that says maybe, maybe not. It is the wick of uncertainty, the candle we light for humility. Scientists defend them like knights: without error bars you are selling propaganda. Yet outside the lab we treat them as shameful, the plus-minus of waist sizes, of salary ranges, of predicted arrival times. We forget they are love letters to honesty. I want to live inside an error bar, declare residence in that liminal whisker where the measurement confesses its own unfinishedness. There the introvert can claim both target and escape, the chef can under-salt and over-spice simultaneously, the poet can print the draft and the masterpiece on the same page. Error bars are the graph’s admission that the world is wider than our rulers. I date them, marry them, grow old between their parentheses. Children will visit and ask, “Which line is real?” I will answer, “All of them, especially the one that dissolves into fog.” I will die somewhere in the upper whisker, above average, short of certainty, carried out feetfirst on a statistical palanquin, my tombstone etched with a tilde: approximately here.

16. Dream in which I am elected treasurer of the birds

Campaign promises: convertible pockets lined with seed, transmutation of cat into shrub. Opponent: a magpie who claims singular vision for shiny objects. Debate stage: the power line behind the Arby’s. I win by one chirp, take office at dawn. My inauguration is a murmuration—thousands shifting like a lung inhaling skyline. Budget is simple: convert all song into currency; pay predators in minor keys; invest surplus south for the winter. Auditors are owls, unimpressed. I balance ledgers on the backs of migrating geese, each V a vector of remittance. Scandal erupts when sparrows allege embezzlement of millet. I resign mid-flight, drop my resignation letter over the interstate where it lands on a windshield, is wiper-bladed into mulch. Legacy: a single bluejay still repeating my slogan—“Crumb for all, all for crumb”—outside the bedroom window each morning, a reminder that even imaginary office leaves real droppings.

17. Letter to the stranger who returned my wallet in 2007

You left it on the windshield under the wiper blade, no note, nothing wet missing. I had been drunk the night before, parading through dive bars pretending adequacy. Inside were twenty-three dollars and a photo of someone I no longer speak to. You could have kept both; instead you secured them against rain with my own parking ticket. I have invented a hundred stories for you: single parent on the way to a double shift, off-duty nun wrestler with a soft spot for fools. The truth is you were blur, a kindness without face, and maybe that is why I cannot forget. I have tried to repay the debt incrementally: tipped fifty percent, dropped coins in expired meters, mailed anonymous twenties to friends who posted受挫 Venmo requests. But the ledger never balances because each act accrues interest in your absence. So I am writing this into the public ether, like slipping a message into a bottle that will never reach its shore. If by chance you recognise yourself—maybe you remember the blue hatchback plastered with bumper stickers quoting Vonnegut—know that your five minutes of integrity metastasized into a lifetime policy. I measure every choice against that mute standard: would the wallet-returner approve? The answer is not always yes, but the question has become my truest compass. May your tires avoid nails, may your groceries stay upright in the parking lot rain, may some future drunk kid fumble your kindness forward until we have built an invisible relay around the world.

18. Meditation on the breath of cities

Every metropolis exhales a signature. Paris: unfiltered Gauloise and bergamot steam from subway grates. Mumbai: diesel marinated in cardamom, a sigh that catches in the throat. Mexico City: avocado diesel, ozone after the下午 storm. New York: roasted nuts plus the graphite of ambition. I collect these exhalations like vintages, store them in the cave of memory where temperature never varies. Arrive in a new place and the first inhale is introduction; by the third day the lungs have signed a lease. The body learns to expect the local cocktail of particulates; departure becomes a small detox. Jet lag is only partly circadian—it is also the lungs grieving the missing molecules. I imagine a future where perfumers sell City Essence: bottled smog to open when homesick. Spritz Lagos in a Tokyo efficiency, inhale the congestion of memory. Conversely, we might send missionaries of pure air to suffocating capitals, canisters of Patagonian breeze released over traffic jams like tear gas in reverse. Until then I practice urban pranayama: stand on the highest bridge, inhale the exhaust of ten million ambitions, exhale my own, a trade as intimate as blood transfusion. The city breathes me as much as I breathe it, an unspoken symbiosis. When I die, scatter my ashes into the rush-hour bloodstream so I may continue commuting through strangers, a speck of carbon in the metropolis that inhaled me whole.

19. The apostrophe conspiracy

Consider the conspiracy of apostrophes: they claim to be letters, yet they hover like surveillance drones. They indicate possession, contraction, sometimes plural confusion—an orthographic Swiss Army knife wielded by grammarian despots. Remove them and the empire does not fall; texts arrive scuffed but legible. Yet we defend them like heirlooms. I propose a guerrilla movement to repurpose the apostrophe as a unit of silence, the inverse of a rest in music. Insert ^ between words and the reader must pause the length of a blink. Soon novels would swell with quiet, sentences like Morse code of hush. Poets would become librarians of the unsaid. The Turkish language already knows the taste of undotted letters; we would merely import the concept of unuttered punctuation. Imagine the dramatic monologue: “I love you^but not enough^to stay.” Each hiatus a small abyss the audience must bridge with their own implication. Over time dictionaries would add the silent apostrophe, defining it as “a gap where meaning breeds.” Linguistic evolution at the speed of reticence. Typographers would design fonts whose apostrophes fade mid-air, ink evaporating like breath on glass. Future scholars would date eras by the amount of white space between words, the way geographers date earthquakes by the shift in fence lines. The final stage: abolish the symbol entirely, leave only the space, a negative carved so deep no grammar can possess it. Then we will have retrieved silence from the margins, made every sentence a collaboration with the unsayable. And in that hush, perhaps we will hear what the apostrophe was guarding all along: the rumor that language is only the echo of a larger quiet.

20. How to survive the forty-third year

Wake up early enough to catch the sky blushing, ashamed of its own beauty. Drink water before coffee, apologize to no one for the order of hydration. Answer emails within twenty-four hours unless they contain the phrase “just circling back,” in which case let them orbit indefinitely. Walk at least one mile with no device, permit the boredom to reformat firmware. Call parents; ask for recipe you will never cook, archive their cadence. Read a poem aloud to the mirror; forgive the reflection for knowing too much. Save twenty percent, give five, waste the rest on fresh berries and impossible theater tickets. Learn the name of one star and pronounce it wrong forever. Keep a plant alive long enough to feel guilty when it dies; compost and begin again. Write the date on the wall behind the bookshelf; resist the urge to google when you painted. Kiss someone who expects nothing; kiss someone who expects everything; notice the different muscles involved. When the knee predicts rain, thank it for prophecy. When the heart flutters like a mis-hit snare, schedule the echocardiogram. Accept that you will Google symptoms at 2 a.m.; accept the verdict of benign. Delete one app that monetizes your insecurity; replace it with metronome. Practice the sentence “I was mistaken” until it fits in your mouth like a familiar coin. Memorise a joke clean enough for nine-year-olds; test on tired cashiers. Vote in small elections; know the name of the person who collects garbage. When nostalgia punches below the belt, counter with future tense: someday I will eat figs in Sicily. When ambition goose-steps, remind it of the mortality rate. At year’s end tally scars like tree rings; note which ones ache in cold, which have become mere illustration. Record voice memo for the sixty-third year: Don’t forget the water, the star, the joke. Save to cloud you do not understand, trust it like you trust the heart’s invisible wiring. Sleep before midnight at least twice a week; dream of nothing specific, of corridors that smell of pencils, of hands folding paper, of bridges made from apostrophes of silence. Wake again. Repeat until the orbit completes, until the calendar peels itself, until the age dissolves into ordinary daylight, indistinguishable from any other day you were lucky enough to receive and reckless enough to claim.

21. Coda at 3:03 a.m.

The word count ticks toward the promised land and the room has cooled to the temperature of thought. My wrists emit the small complaint of ligaments overused, like loyal dogs asked to fetch one last stick even as the horizon purples with dawn. Outside, a delivery truck backs up, beeping the Morse of commerce. I have written myself a scavenger hunt across two decades, stitched scent to sound to socket to sorrow, and still the universe feels roomy, capacious, a hotel where vacancies outnumber guests. If you have read this far, we share a conspiracy of insomnia, or curiosity, or both. Let us agree to meet again in some other paragraph, perhaps one that smells of cedar and loose teeth, or of city breath after rain. Until then, keep a mismatched sock in your pocket as passport, whistle with two fingers when lost, remember that apostrophes once conspired to teach us silence. The screen flickers; my coffee is cold; the fireflies of childhood have upgraded to LED but still blink. I click save, not out of certainty, but courtesy—to the future insomniac, to the past child, to the error bars that wick our lives. The document settles into its digital folder like a paper crane on a windowsill, waiting for wind, for fingers, for whatever mercy morning decides to extend.

samples.prompt: Write freely about whatever you want for 2500 words.
samples.result:
Alright—let’s take the hinges off the door and let the room breathe.  
No outline, no map, no GPS voice telling me to make a legal U-turn in 500 feet.  
Just the sound of fingertips on keys, the faint smell of coffee gone cold, and the sense that somewhere a clock is ticking but refusing to show its face.  
I could warn you that what follows will wander, but you already know that; you asked for 2,500 words of whatever, which is a little like handing someone a passport and saying, “Surprise me.”  
So I will.  
I’ll start with the small ceramic pot on my windowsill that is supposed to grow dwarf basil but instead grows a single confident dandelion.  
I never planted it; it arrived overnight, a stowaway in the shrink-wrapped soil, and now it stands there like a punk singer at a church picnic—loud, defiant, entirely out of but somehow perfect.  
Every morning I raise the blinds and we lock eyes: me, puffy and caffeine-deprived; it, yellow and audacious.  
I can’t bring myself to evict it.  
Eviction implies authority, and authority implies a plan, and I have no plan for this pot, this plant, this life.  
The dandelion, meanwhile, has a plan so simple it barely deserves the word: open, drink light, make seeds, riot.  
I envy it the clarity.  

I read once that astronauts on the International Space Station sometimes spend their scarce free time simply staring out the cupola windows at Earth rolling beneath them.  
They do not, as Hollywood promised, spend the whole time bounding through zero-g like toddlers in a ball pit, hollering about science.  
They hover, chin on padded rail, and watch the Nile unspool in brown silk, watch auroras lick the pole like cosmic cats, watch lightning scribble urgent Morse on the night side of the planet.  
Weeks later, back aboard Terra Firma, some of them report a low-grade, persistent ache that medicine can’t name.  
Not muscle atrophy, not calcium loss—something subtler.  
They miss, they say, the view.  
Not the grandeur so much as the forced perspective: being able to see the whole story at once, the blue-white epilogue wrapped in a paper-thin membrane of air you could poke with a gloved finger.  
Down here, gravity herds us into narrow sequences; we see the strip-mall sign but not the desert, the tweet but not the century.  
From orbit, context is gratuitous, almost cruel in its generosity.  
I think about that when I scroll headlines at 2 a.m., when the world feels like a fistfight in a phone booth.  
I imagine folding myself into a SpaceX capsule and climbing until all the screaming coalesces into a silent, swirling marble.  
Then I remember I get motion sickness on a Ferris wheel, and also that space is booked solid months in advance, and also that dwarf basil is still dead, dandelion undead, and the clock is still hiding.  
Perspective is a luxury item; the rest of us pay retail.  

Speaking of retail, last week I walked into a big-box store at 9:47 p.m.—the fluorescent half-light of almost-closing, when the piped-in music drops to a guilty whisper and employees glide past like courteous ghosts.  
I needed, I told myself, a single AA battery.  
What I really needed was to stand inside a cathedral of options, to be a body among aisles of possible lives.  
Spring collection grilling accoutrements glinted at me; pastel ottomans waved; an end-cap display hawked motion-activated toilet-bowl lights that promised “16 million colors.”  
Capitalism at its most deliriously sincere.  
I zig-zagged, touching textures: the rubbery lure of a phone case that looks like a toasted slice of bread, the velvet promise of a “pom-pom throw” in a shade named “driftwood daydream.”  
Everything was both essential and absurd, like placeholder dialogue in a dream script: “Buy me, and you will finally.”  
Finally what?  
The sentence never completes; it just dangles, tempting, like a carrot made of fog.  
Eventually my hand found the battery rack.  
I stood there holding a four-pack that cost less than the latte I’d choked down that morning, and I felt the absurd helium leak out of me.  
Four tiny cylinders, each wearing a shiny tuxedo of zinc, capable of energizing a smoke detector, a sex toy, a cello tuner, a coup d’état.  
I paid, walked out under the insect-zapper buzz of parking-lot lamps, and drove home past shuttered churches and 24-hour car washes glowing like secular temples.  
Back in my kitchen I installed one battery in a cheap kitchen scale, threw the remaining three into a drawer already bristling with orphaned batteries, twist-ties, and a plastic banana that plays “Despacito” when squeezed.  
The drawer is my personal museum of almost-use, a humid taxonomy of little urgencies that forgot to announce themselves solved.  
Sometimes I open it just to confirm I’m the same person who once believed each of those objects was the hinge upon which a new life would swing.  

Now that I think of it, drawers are secret diaries written in object instead of verb.  
My grandmother kept one drawer in her walnut highboy dedicated entirely to hosiery, still in the package, size B, suntan, “sheer elegance” promised on a cardboard band that looked like it had survived wartime rationing.  
She never wore them—preferred knee-highs from the drugstore—but she bought a fresh pair every few years, as though some formal occasion might storm the gates and demand unblemished legs.  
When she died we found seventeen unopened pairs stacked like communion wafers.  
I took one, not to wear but to keep, a soft time capsule.  
It sits now in my own drawer-of-misfit-desires, side by side with a Matchbox car whose right front wheel is missing, a Canadian five-dollar bill featuring a younger queen, and the stub of a ticket to a 1998 Smashing Pumpkins show where I kissed a girl whose last name I never learned, only that she tasted like cherry slush and panic.  
Objects don’t mourn, but they do persist; that is their quiet revenge on flesh.  

Mid-typing, thunder slips across the roof like a mover dragging furniture.  
The dandelion trembles, broadcast-ready.  
Rain starts—no polite sprinkle, but the sky’s abrupt decision to dump the equivalent of unread emails onto my yard.  
I crack the window anyway because I love the smell: petrichor, that mongrel of rock and root, oil and ozone.  
It reminds me of being eight and barefoot on summer pavement, racing the neighbor kid to the corner store for popsicles that cost a quarter and melted faster than you could unwrap them.  
We’d stand on the curb licking neon runoff from our knuckles while the first drops sizzled on the blacktop, turning it into a city of mirrored streets where upside-down clouds parked themselves.  
Back then weather felt like a conspiracy designed for us alone; we never checked a radar, never asked why storm clouds resembled bruises.  
Now every front is plotted, named, tweeted.  
The National Hurricane Center assigns official fonts to disaster.  
Sometimes knowledge is just another layer of glass between you and the rain.  

I wonder what happened to that neighbor kid.  
His name was Jordan McKnight, no relation to the singer, and he could whistle through the gap in his teeth loud enough to summon dogs from three blocks away.  
We lost touch after his parents divorced and his mom moved them to a town named, improbably, Normal.  
“Going to Normal,” he told me, pocketing a final popsicle stick, as though relocating to a temperament rather than a municipality.  
I picture him now in some subdivision garage, tuning a lawn mower engine, married to a woman who sells essential oils on Facebook.  
Maybe he still whistles, maybe the gap closed after braces, maybe he remembers the storms.  
Maybe he’s dead; statistics allow for that.  
I could search online, but I fear the disappointment of discovering nothing—or worse, something too complete: wife, kids, politics, a LinkedIn headline.  
Mystery is the last intact popsicle; leave it in the wrapper.  

Rain thickens; the gutter gurgles like an old man clearing his throat.  
I should be doing things—fiscal chores, correspondence, the perpetual dishes that breed in the sink like fruit flies plotting a coup.  
Instead I stand here half-hypnotized, thinking about the word “gutter.”  
Such a blunt, honest syllable.  
In German it’s *Rinne*, which sounds like something you could keep crackers in.  
In Spanish, *canaleta*, almost musical, a diminutive canal.  
But English insists on the thud of “gutter,” the same ufft your shoe makes when it discovers a squirrel’s half-eaten funeral.  
I love language for its refusal to be fair: why does “river” sound like shiver, while “stream” manages a lullaby?  
Why does “love” rhyme with “dove” but never with “prove”?  
We are governed by accidents that got promoted to rules.  
Meanwhile somewhere in the world a child is learning to speak and will one day say “I hurted myself” and be corrected by an adult who says “I hurt myself,” thereby initiating the kid into the grand human project of pretending grammar is logical.  
Eventually the same kid will type “irregardless” and be shamed online by someone wearing a T-shirt that reads “I’m silently correcting your grammar.”  
The circle of linguistic life, scored by a chorus of teeth.  

Speaking of circles, have you noticed how modern life is a series of escalating them?  
The scroll wheel on your mouse spins infinitely outward, yet you end up back at the top tweet.  
The washing machine’s dial turns clockwise through “Normal,” “Heavy,” “Delicate,” then lands again on “Normal,” as though it’s trying to nudge you toward existential acceptance.  
Even recipes now insist you “circle back” to taste, to season, to serve.  
I picture some mid-level manager of vocabulary sitting in a fluorescent dungeon, paid per diem to insert “circle back” into every memo until language folds in on itself like a Möbius strip made of jargon.  
We are all just dogs chasing our own tails, except the tail is made of meetings and the dog is overtime-exempt.  

At some point the rain yields to the kind of hush that feels like the sky is catching its breath.  
A single bird—species unknown, talent undeniable—starts a call that ricochets from rooftop to rooftop, a lone investor whistling the market upward.  
Birdsong is GPS for the soul; you can’t help but triangulate your position within the grid of Now.  
I read once that European blackcaps have altered migration routes because backyard bird feeders now offer reliable winter snacks.  
Basically, we’ve invented bird Airbnb.  
Evolution used to be a glacial committee; now it’s a side hustle.  
I wonder if millennia from now avian archaeologists—improbable phrase—will unearth plastic feeders and theorize weird rituals: “Humans poured seeds into elevated shrines to atone for glass-window collisions.”  
They won’t be entirely wrong.  

I lose track of how long I’ve been typing.  
The laptop battery icon gutters—there’s that word again—red at 9 %.  
The AA cells in the drawer seem to smirk: “Wrong form factor, pal.”  
I ignore them and keep pushing letters into the void.  
There’s an illicit thrill in writing without destination, like sailing off the edge of a flat-earth map and discovering not doom but more ocean.  
Every artist secretly wants to be a lighthouse keeper: stand on a cliff, fling signals into dark, hope some ship blinks back.  
Most of the time, though, you’re just talking to the moon, which is notorious for acting aloof until it isn’t, waxing dramatic, waning indifferent.  
Still we signal.  

Let’s talk about the moon for a second.  
Earth’s moon is 400 times smaller than the Sun, but the Sun is 400 times farther away, so voilà: total solar eclipses possible.  
That’s the cosmological equivalent of a party trick nobody planned.  
If the numbers were off by even a sliver, we’d never get that black-velvet crown, that coronal halo photographers chase like lovesick teens.  
I stood in the path of totality once, in a Nebraska cornfield flanked by strangers who, for two minutes, became my family.  
When the light collapsed into a 360-degree sunset, when crickets struck up their night instruments and Venus appeared like a shy chaperone, I heard a man sob—just one, raw gulp, as though the universe had slid a love letter under his door.  
We all pretended not to notice, but we were crying too, inside our skulls, where no one can copyright the view.  
Then daylight resurrected, and the cornfield remembered it was private property, and we scattered to our respective sedans, back to podcasts and gas-station bathrooms.  
Still, the afterimage lingered like a first kiss you can’t quite tell friends about.  

Perspective again: astronauts, eclipses, dandelions.  
You asked for whatever, and apparently whatever is a constellation of attempts to locate oneself in a cosmos allergic to maps.  
Fine by me.  
I once tried to meditate—properly, on a cushion sewed by Nepalese nuns, scented candles flickering like guilt.  
The instructor said, “Let thoughts come and go like clouds.”  
Lovely metaphor until you remember clouds can storm, can flood basement apartments, can kill power for a week.  
My thoughts arrived dressed as unpaid invoices, then morphed into high-school humiliations, then into a dream I’d had at age six where my toys walked into the ocean and never waved goodbye.  
By the time the gong sounded I was a knot of grief and caffeine, plus one numb foot.  
I left there certain that mindfulness is just voyeurism aimed inward, and I’m not sure I have consent.  

Cold coffee finally meets my lips, tastes like liquid cardboard.  
The bird has been joined by a backup choir; the street is dripping percussion.  
I feel the essay approaching the far side of its allotted acres, like a hike that’s gone satisfyingly sore.  
2,500 words is a strange distance—farther than a sprint, shorter than a migration.  
You can fit a couple small confessions, a rant or two, maybe one decent metaphor before the countdown hits zero.  
I could fact-check myself, but that would break whatever spell lets me type “cosmological equivalent of a party trick” without deleting in shame.  
Shame is the tax we pay for pretending we’re coherent.  
I prefer incoherence with pockets of radiance, like a citywide blackout during which certain apartments still insist on hosting jazz.  

If you’ve read this far, congratulations: you’ve achieved the stamina usually reserved for binge-watching true-crime documentaries.  
You may be wondering, “Where is this going?”  
The honest answer is: it’s already there.  
Like a river, the only place left to go is onward, even if onward circles.  
I could end with a pithy maxim—something about enjoying the journey, or watering plastic plants, or whistling through dental gaps—but maxims are just fortune cookies minus the calories, and I’m not hungry.  
Instead, I’ll offer an image:  

Picture the dandelion again, now fully awake after rain.  
Each pollen shaft wears a miniature droplet, a contact lens refracting the backyard into tiny planets.  
A single ant scales the stalk, feelers coding signals we’ll never decrypt.  
Somewhere overhead a passenger jet inscribes a white line that will vanish before you can decide if it’s ominous or hopeful.  
Time, that old plagiarist, keeps self-duplicating, but the ant doesn’t unionize, the dandelion doesn’t file flight plans, and the gutter keeps its mouth open to the sky.  
Nothing resolves; everything participates.  
All you can do is stand at the window, fingertips on cool glass, and try to send a postcard into the moment:  
“Wish you were here.  
Actually, you are.  
Love, Whoever.”

samples.prompt: Write freely about whatever you want for 2500 words.
samples.result:
The moon is a hole.

Not the kind you fall through, but the kind you fall out of. I’ve been thinking about this for years, ever since the night I stood on the roof of my parents’ house in the Mojave, sixteen and trying not to smoke the whole Camel in front of the Milky Way. The tiles were still warm from the day’s 104 degrees; the tar beneath them exhaled slowly like a sleeping animal. Somewhere below, the AC unit kicked on with a mechanical sigh, and the sound rose up the st stucco walls and mixed with the crickets until machine and insect became one organism. I looked at the moon—just past full, bruised slightly on one edge—and felt it looking back. Not with the face people like to draw, not the Man, not the Rabbit, but with a rim: a thin silver lip around dark. A hole, I thought. A hole that goes all the way through whatever sky is made of and comes out somewhere else. I held that thought the way you hold a marble in your mouth, rolling it against the teeth, tasting glass.

Years later, in a different desert, I would stand in the Namib under a new moon and understand that darkness can also be a passage. The guides had told us to turn off our headlamps if we wanted to see the stars, so I did, and the world re-made itself into scattered salt on black porcelain. I thought: if the moon is a hole, then absence is the door. You don’t open it; you notice it already gapes. The sky was so clear that satellites soldered their tiny threads across the dome, and I felt the old vertigo return—same roof, same tar, same sixteen—only now the house was my body, and the stars were the neighbors I never met.

I keep trying to write about this, but language keeps slipping off. Words are coins dropped into deep water; they float for a second, glint, then tilt nose-down and sink. What I want is not to describe the hole but to lean far enough forward that I fall in, and I want you—reader, stranger, future self—to fall too, so we meet inside whatever chamber lives past the lip. I don’t know what we’ll find. Maybe a silence shaped like a cathedral organ. Maybe an ocean that sings in frequencies whales haven’t invented yet. Maybe only the echo of our own breathing, boxed and shipped back to us by distance. That would be enough. Breathing is the first story; everything else is commentary.

When I was seven I had a tin astronaut, three inches tall, painted silver with a red visor and a backpack the size of a Chiclet. His name changed weekly: Armstrong, then Aldrin, then Buzz Lightyear before I knew Buzz Lightyear existed. I used to stand him on the windowsill at night so he could “keep watch.” Over what, I didn’t know. Over me, I guess. Over the dark. One night I woke and found him lying on his back, helmet cracked. No earthquake, no prowler—just gravity and time. I buried him in the backyard between two zucchini plants that would later balloon into alien lifeforms of their own. The funeral was brief: a popsicle-stick cross, a hymn hummed through closed teeth. Years afterward I pictured that figurine underground, paint flaking, metallic skin going the color of abandoned shopping carts. I hoped the corrosion looked like starlight close-up: a slow sparkle. I hoped the earth around him felt like launch.

We are all launched, eventually. Puberty is a first-stage rocket: awkward, loud, impossible to steer. Love is the second: fuel burns faster than you expect, and suddenly the capsule wobbles. Grief is the moment staging occurs—metal separates with a sound like a throat clearing—and you look out the window and see the planet you knew shrinking to a swirl of white and turquoise. After that, reentry is just geography. People talk about “holding space,” but nobody mentions that space is already mostly holes. Hold a hole long enough and your hands forget the difference between empty and full.

I want to talk about my grandmother’s thimbles, but first I have to explain that she never called them thimbles. She called them “finger hats,” which made my brother giggle so hard the first time he heard it that milk punched through his nose and spackled the kitchen linoleum. She kept the finger hats in a short glass jar that once held store-brand peaches halves. The jar lived on the windowsill above the sink, catching sun the way a cat traps heat. When she sewed—mostly to reattach buttons my grandfather popped during Sunday dinners—she would choose a silver one, slip it on, and suddenly her hand became a knight wearing a tiny, useless helmet. I liked to imagine the thimble embarking on quests across the tablecloth: rescuing loose threads, jousting with lint. After she died, my mother packed the jar in newspaper and drove it to Goodwill. I still don’t know why that felt like a second funeral. Maybe because objects are just time that has agreed to hold still. When the jar disappeared, the minutes inside it were released, and they scattered like startled birds. Sometimes I find one of those minutes lodged in an ordinary hour—while I’m buttering toast, say—and I have to sit down until the fluttering stops.

There is a kind of dream that does not happen in sleep. You’re walking the cereal aisle, comparing oat bluster to honey crunch, and suddenly you remember a place you have never been: a wooden jetty, tide slapping pylons, gulls arguing overhead. The memory arrives complete with salt on your lips, although you’re landlocked, although it’s February. You pause between the Cheerios and the off-brand loops, invaded by longing for water you’ve never touched. These dreams are postcards sent from parallel lives, and the postmark is always smudged. Scientists call them “involuntary autobiographical memories,” which is science’s way of admitting it, too, is homesick for somewhere. I think of them as moon-light: reflected, secondhand, but bright enough to cast a shadow. I collect them the way children collect smooth glass, pocketing even the chipped pieces. One day I will build a mosaic of almost-places, grout them together with the silt of jetty-dreams, and erect the whole thing in an unused corner of the sky. Visitors will stand on my jetty that goes nowhere and feel the vertigo of being exiled from an event that never occurred. They will take off their shoes, because that’s what you do when the ground is imaginary but the salt is real.

Have you ever tried to mail a potato? Not a postcard of a potato, but the tuber itself, licked and stamped and shoved into the slot. A friend did this once—scribbled an address on the skin with Sharpie, paid parcel rate, and sent it 400 miles. It arrived wrinkled, astonished, smelling of earth and adhesive. The post office had stamped it “NON-MACHINABLE,” which is how I feel most days: unable to be automatically sorted. The recipient cooked it anyway, peeled away the ink along with the address, added butter, cream, nostalgia. “Tastes like correspondence,” she said. I keep meaning to try it myself, but I worry the potato would arrive minus its message, or worse—bearing someone else’s. Imagine a potato inscribed with a love letter meant for another mouth. You mash it and suddenly find yourself proposing to a stranger. Marriage conducted entirely through starches: the original covenant of carbohydrates. The divorce is just hunger.

The first time I saw the ocean I was already twenty-three, which is criminal for someone raised in a state that owns more coastline than jealousy. But deserts kept getting in the way: the Mojave, the Great Basin, the Sonoran, the semester I dropped out and drove to Tucson because a girl said she liked my handwriting. When I finally reached the Pacific, it was January and the water was the color of dishwasher rinse. I took off my shoes, rolled my jeans, and stepped in. The cold was a cathedral. I remember thinking, So this is the thing that practiced on the moon before visiting Earth: tides, the original rehearsal for gravity’s tug. I kept wading until the hem of my coat drank salt. A wave slapped my thigh like an auntie who hasn’t seen you since you were “this high.” Somewhere behind me, gulls performed unlicensed karaoke of my thoughts. I realized I was crying, but in a manner so covert I didn’t discover until the tears reached my mouth. Salt recognizing salt. The moon that night was only a toenail clipping, but the hole was still there, inverted now: instead of silver edge around dark, a dark edge around silver. I understood that every exit is also an entrance, depending which side of the door you’re calling home.

I want to inventory small salvations. One: The smell of library books—dust and sugar mixed, like ghosts eating toast. Two: The precise click of a pepper grinder when you know the pasta is almost ready. Three: Highway signs that warn of wandering animals for the next seventeen miles, as if the landscape itself might stray. Four: The way a friend's voice tilts on the phone when she says “I was just thinking about you,” and you both know it’s true because the universe keeps a petty cash drawer of coincidences for exactly these purchases. Five: The moment you realize you have survived the thing you swore would kill you, and the knowledge arrives so quiet you almost miss it—like noticing the fridge light only when it fails to turn on. Six: The fact that the word “helicopter” is not heli-scope + -ter, but helico- (spiral) + -pter (wing), which means every time a helicopter lifts, a small spiral is tightened into the sky, a spring you cannot see but trust to uncoil in an emergency. Seven: The way your mother still clips recipes she will never cook, because hope is a casserole that never quite bakes. Eight: The discovery that the etymology of “comfort” is com- (together) + -fortis (strong), so comfort is literally the strength that comes from being together, which is why solitude can feel like weakness even when you chose it. Nine: The knowledge that somewhere a stranger is humming the exact same song stuck in your molars, and the air between you vibrates like a string you both share but will never pluck. Ten: The moon, the hole, the door, the breath, the next word.

Let’s talk about breath. Not the metaphysical kind—“breath of life,” “breath of fresh air”—but the actual wet exchange between your lungs and the planet. Every inhalation is a small loan from the troposphere; every exhalation returns interest in carbon dioxide. You are in reciprocal debt with trees. Close your eyes and picture the accounting: a ledger floating above your head like a transparent halo, numbers flickering. You owe the Amazon roughly 2.3 million breaths; a jacaranda in a suburban cul-de-sac carries a smaller share, more of a payday loan. The ocean owns the majority of your outstanding breaths because plankton works overtime. When people say “I can’t breathe,” they are describing a foreclosure. George Floyd’s last words were a balance sheet ripped out of the bank and set on fire. I think of this every time I step outside and taste the breeze that carries jacaranda petals, pepper trees, the faint diesel of the bus route. I try to inhale slowly, as if interest compounds less on a cautious withdrawal. It doesn’t. The only way to settle the debt is to green something—plant, protect, vote, repair. Until then, we huff and puff and wonder why storms feel personal. They are. Every hurricane is a collections agency.

I keep a list of unfinished letters. Not love letters—those are a different species—but letters to companies, officials, the editor, my past. They begin politely (“Dear Sirs, I am writing to inquire…”) and then detour into autobiography, indictment, weather reports. They end mid-sentence, usually at the point where I realize I am angry about something I cannot name, something shaped like a hole. The cursor sits blinking, a metronome for a song I haven’t written. I save them anyway, farm of half-things. One day I will harvest the openings and stitch them into a single enormous preamble: the prologue to a life that finally starts once the complaining is done. I picture readers skipping that section, jumping straight to chapter one where the hero is already on the road, pockets light, heart optimized. But I will know the preamble existed, the way a beach knows every stone you never skipped.

Let’s consider the etymology of “consider”: con- (together) + -sidus (stars). To consider is literally to bring the stars together, to consult the sky before deciding. Every choice you make under a roof is secretly moderated by constellations you cannot see. This is why skylights feel like honesty. This is why cities, gelled in their own sodium glow, still suffer a rash of indecisions: they forgot the stars. I try to consult them when I can. I drive twenty miles out, kill the headlights, let the dark rush into the cab like displaced water. The stars re-order themselves into a parliament. I lay on the warm hood and present my agenda: Should I move? Should I forgive? Should I plant jacarandas or olives? They answer in their ancient language: silence seasoned with distance. I decode it as “maybe,” which is stellar for “you already know, but we’ll watch.” I accept their terms. The drive back is always shorter, as if the car coasts on diminished doubt.

Now the word count pushes 2,200, and I feel the same pressure I felt at sixteen on the roof: the need to conclude before the tar cools, before my mother notices the cigarette smell, before the moon sets and takes its hole with it. But conclusions are fake currency. The moon will rise tomorrow, still punctured, still silver-lipped. The loan officer of breath will keep sending envelopes. The helicopter will tighten its spring. The unfinished letters will coplate, generate new white space, reproduce. The potato will sprout eyes in some dark pantry and begin to write its own address in pale roots. My grandmother’s finger hats are somewhere on a stranger’s thumb, pressing fabric together, keeping the world from unraveling thread by thread. And you—yes, you, who have followed me this far— you are now holding a piece of my debt to the trees. Spend it wisely: inhale, exhale, plant something.

I began with a hole and end with a seed. That is the shape of most stories if you track them upside-down. The moon is a hole; the seed is a moon reversed. Plant it tonight. Wait. While you wait, consider the stars, mail a potato, hum a song someone else is humming, bury a toy where zucchini can guard it. Forgive the freeway for existing; forgive yourself for using it. When the green shoot appears, whisper to it the password: “comfort.” Together, strong. Together, strong. The breath you borrow will be returned. The hole you enter will be exited. The word you needed was the last one you already said, but say it again, say it aloud: moon, hole, seed, yes.

samples.prompt: Write freely about whatever you want for 2500 words.
samples.result:
The first thing to notice is the smell: not a single note but a chord struck in the nose—iron, yeast, burnt sugar, wet concrete, diesel, living sap, dead leaf. It is 6:17 a.m. in the city that insists on calling itself “the city” even though everyone who lives here knows cities are plural, multiplied inside each other like nested dolls. I am walking uphill toward the river with no intention of arriving; the point is to keep the body moving so the mind can sit still inside the motion, the way a sparrow sits inside a swaying branch. I have given myself permission to write whatever arrives for two thousand five hundred words, which is approximately the number of steps it takes to reach the water, cross the footbridge, double back along the warehouse district, climb the fire-escape stairs to the promenade, and descend again. I have done this walk so often that my calves remember it better than my memory; they twitch in anticipation of corners the way horses twitch at invisible flies. The city is still half asleep, but the city is also never asleep, so what I mean is that the human layer is thin right now, the layer that speaks in mortgage rates and brunch reservations. Beneath it the older city breathes: pallets clatter, bread rises, barges argue with the tide, foxes slip through gaps in chain-link like red smoke.

I decide—because on a walk you are always deciding and undecething—not to bring my phone. I leave it facedown on the kitchen table like a small black coffin. Immediately I feel the phantom limb: the pocket pat, the thumb swipe at absent glass. Without the map I will have to navigate by nostalgia, which is more reliable than satellites and also more vindictive. Already I am lost inside a sentence, which is another way of saying found. Language is my true GPS, each vowel a coordinate, each consonant a curb to trip over. I whisper “con-son-ant” and hear the word’s own metallic click, the way it cons the son, the way it insists on staying even after the meaning has left the room.

Behind me a garbage truck performs its daily miracle: it eats what we reject and yet we call it trash, not prophecy. The men on the back wear neon vests the color of highlighter pens; they move in choreographed hunger, grabbing, flinging, slamming. One of them has headphones on; I can hear the tiny insect tick of his music, a hi-hat like a nail being driven into distant wood. He nods at me, a miniature bow, and I feel honored, the way one feels honored when a wild animal acknowledges you without fleeing. Respect is sometimes measured in the absence of fear.

At the first corner I encounter the bakery that does not open for another hour but has already started its exhalation. The smell of fermenting dough is almost alcoholic. I imagine the baker inside, a woman named Elena whom I have never seen but who signs her confections in icing like a magician initialing a dove. She is probably shaping brioche, tucking the edges like a mother smoothing a blanket over a sleeping child. I once read that the same strain of yeast used in San Francisco sourdough has been circulating since 1849, a microscopic dynasty. Every bite is therefore a form of time travel, a souvenir from the gold rush dissolved in saliva. I picture a miner in flannel, his teeth sinking into tomorrow’s breakfast, the sour tang reminding him that some things outlast nuggets and most things do not. I store this thought in the cheek of my mind like a hard candy to suck later.

The light at this hour is a liar. It pretends blue impartiality but sneakily gilds everything it touches: a crushed energy-drink can becomes a tiny mosque dome; a puddle of motor oil turns into a black mirror where the sky practices frowning. I pass a mural of a woman whose face has been replaced by weather. One cheek is sun, one cheek rain, her mouth a bolt of lightning so bright it has faded to graphite. Underneath, someone has spray-painted in dripping magenta: “I AM NOT YOUR METAPHOR.” The sentence feels pointed at me personally, the way a stranger’s cough on the bus feels pointed. I nod apology to the wall and keep walking. Artists hate being useful; they want to remain feral.

Word count: 517. I know because I have been counting footfalls, averaging four words per step, a nerdy pilgrim’s rosary. The contract I signed with myself says I may not cheat by stuffing filler; every word must be a real immigrant arriving at the border of the sentence, passport in hand, hoping to stay. I allow myself pauses, the way breathallows itself commas. A sparrow lands on the rim of a trash can and fixes me with one bead-eye. I imagine it saying, “You think you're observing, but I am the real observer here, noting how you anthropomorphize to avoid loneliness.” I bow again. Birds are the last honest critics.

Block quote: “The city is a text written in sidewalk gum.” I scribble this on an imaginary Post-it and stick it to the inside of my skull. Already the gum has accumulated stories: the freshman who kissed for the first time and panicked at the taste of menthol; the construction worker who stepped in it and left a tiny map of tread marks; the pigeon that mistook a pink wad for shrimp and still walks around with culinary trust issues. We dismiss these blobs as litter, but they are memoirs boiled down to chewable size.

I reach the avenue where the streetlights are still on despite the sky’s growing brightness, stubborn night-shift workers clocking overtime. A cyclist passes, neon vest flapping like a flag for a country that believes in arriving sweaty. His tires hiss, the sound of a librarian shushing a rowdy ghost. He leaves a wake of air that smells like espresso and cedar, probably from the courier box strapped to his back. I picture the contents: a single envelope containing divorce papers, or maybe a birthday card with five dollars inside, the most democratic amount of money—too small to corrupt, too large to ignore. The cyclist disappears and takes his narrative possibilities with him, the way a magician pockets a borrowed watch.

I cross the street diagonally, jaywalking in front of no traffic, a petty rebellion. On the opposite curb lies a single gold glitter high-heel shoe, size impossible to determine because it is bent at an angle Achilles would envy. No mate in sight. I invent three backstories and refuse to choose:

1. Cinderella 2.0, app-based, lost it fleeing when the algorithm struck midnight.
2. A drag queen weaponized it during a dance-off, flung in triumph and forgotten in the afterglow of applause.
3. It fell out of a garbage bag during an eviction, the last glittery thing the tenant refused to abandon until physics insisted.

I photograph it with my mind’s camera, which has unlimited storage but unreliable focus. The shoe gleams like a small insult to gravity and poverty at once.

Word count: 1,012. The hill is steeper now; my sentences shorten like breath. I pass the hospital where my daughter was born eight years ago. The memory arrives uninvited, the way a song arrives when you haven’t heard it since childhood but somehow every lyric is still on the tip of your tongue. I remember the hallway smell—disinfectant trying and failing to cover the deeper smell of human threshold. A nurse had said, “You’ll forget the pain,” which was technically true because trauma keeps the receipt but loses the itemized list. My daughter’s first cry sounded like a tiny hinge opening somewhere inside my chest that I didn’t know was there. I did not cry; I narrated. “She is here,” I said aloud, as if the room required subtitles. Now she is eight and hates wearing socks with seams across the toes; she arranges her stuffed animals in order of cuteness, a ruthless dictator of plush. Time is a pudding you can’t unbake.

I keep walking because that is the contract of the essay and also of life. At the top of the hill I encounter a man asleep on a bus bench, wrapped in a sleeping bag the color of municipal regret. His shoes are placed neatly beside him, laces tucked in like sushi rolls. A cardboard sign reads: “NOT LAZY, JUST TIRED.” The sentence is a haiku without season word. I drop a dollar coin into his hat even though he is not asking; the coin lands with a hush, a small moon falling into silent orbit. I want to say something but language feels dangerous, like bringing a candle into a gunpowder room. Instead I keep moving, the way you keep moving after you drop a stone into a well and wait for the splash that may never come.

The descent begins. Gravity becomes collaborator rather than enemy. My knees negotiate terms. I pass a playground still empty except for one woman pushing a child in a bucket swing. The child wears a helmet, not for protection but because she likes the way it makes her head feel “hugged,” the mother explains to invisible jury. I wave at the girl; she waves back with the flapping wrist of someone learning semaphore. Her joy is pre-grammatical, a burp of pure meaning. I envy her future, the thousands of firsts she does not yet know are coming: first lie, first kiss, first error message when the server is down and love is not.

Word count: 1,507. The river appears abruptly, a silver cliché. It is tidal here, so the water wears a necklace of flotsom: a tennis ball, half a flip-flop, a prescription bottle with the label washed blank. Tides are the moon’s diary, entries rewritten twice daily. I step onto the footbridge and synchronize my footfalls with the pedestrian grooves worn into the wood. Midspan I stop, violating commuter code, and look down. The water is brown until it is silver until it is blue, depending on the angle of your politics. A cormorant surfaces with a twig in its beak, mistaking it for fish, or perhaps collecting building material for a nest it will never finish, like my novel. The bird stares at me, water beading on its neck like tiny transparent medals. I apologize for plastics, for treaties broken in rooms it will never enter. It dives again, taking my apology to the place where apologies dissolve into salt.

I think about how bridges are the only part of infrastructure that willingly admit they are in-between. Roads pretend destination, tunnels pretend permanence, but bridges name their liminality and charge tolls for it. I pay in sentences.

On the far bank warehouses rise like enormous cardboard boxes that got wet and dried into immovable curves. Their windows are broken in deliberate patterns, modernist stained glass for a congregation of pigeons. Someone has painted a new mural overnight: a giant QR code that, when scanned, plays a voicemail of a stranger laughing for thirty-seven seconds straight. I do not scan it; I prefer mystery to comedy. Laugh tracks are the cicadas of human sadness, emerging in regular cycles to remind us we are not alone in finding nothing funny.

I turn left, tracing the river’s edge where the path is cracked like a phone screen still working. Weeds push through: plantain, sow thistle, chickweed, names that sound like medieval insults. They are immigrant flora, stowaways in ship ballast, now citizens paying rent in sunlight. I praise their resilience by stepping around them, a small diplomacy of soles. A rat watches from a storm-drain, tail curved like a question mark no one asked. We lock eyes; I blink first, defeated by mammalian nerve.

Word count: 1,923. Time has begun to stretch the way taffy does when you pull it slowly, becoming thinner but longer. I am not tired; I am elongated. I pass a café whose chalkboard advertises “Depresso: espresso served with a sigh.” I want to order one but the door is locked, the owner inside counting yesterdays in the till. Instead I inhale the ghost of ground beans and keep walking, the way you keep living after someone says goodbye.

Now the route bends inland, uphill again, streets named after trees that were cut down to make room for streets. I climb through layers of neighborhood: first the Portuguese bakery smelling of custard, then the Nigerian hair-braiding shop with mannequin heads staring like detached oracles, then the vegan donut place where the scent of coconut bacon is a moral argument. Each block is a stanza in a poem that refuses to end on a couplet. At the corner a boy sells lemonade from a crate stand painted with the words “COLLEGE FUND OR BUST.” His price is twenty-five cents or best offer; I give him a dollar and decline change. He says, “Thanks, mister, you just bought me half a textbook.” I want to tell him textbooks will be obsolete by the time he arrives, but optimism is a seed that grows best when unwatered by cynicism. I keep climbing.

My calves announce a policy of resistance. I negotiate: only three more blocks, then freedom, then ice, then elevation. The mind bargains with the body the way governments bargain with hostages, promising everything and delivering minutes. I crest the ridge and the city reveals itself all at once, a scatterplot of ambition and defeat. From here the skyline looks like a graph of someone trying to quit smoking: ups, downs, a few towers standing in for relapse. I sit on a bench donated by a family whose plaque reads “IN MEMORY OF HAROLD, WHO LOVED THIS VIEW.” Harold, I decide, was a man who understood that looking is a form of ownership safer than buying. I borrow his bench and his view, keep neither, thank both.

Word count: 2,251. The final descent. My sentences feel like coins spent into an arcade game that gives no tickets, only the lights and sounds of effort. I pass a church whose marquee reads: “GOD IS ALSO TIRED, COME SIT WITH HIM.” I consider it, but I am already sitting inside this paragraph, which is its own kind of pew. I keep going. Gravity now assists; my feet threaten to outrun me. I think about how every walk is a rehearsal for the last walk, the one we won’t remember finishing. I picture the city compiling my data: gait speed, hesitation loops, smell samples, sentence cadence. Big Data as sidewalk gum, sticking to everything.

I arrive back at my front door exactly 2,498 words in my head. I allow myself two more:

The end.

samples.prompt: Write freely about whatever you want for 2500 words.
samples.result:
I want towrite about the moment I realized how little I actually know.

It happened on an otherwise ordinary Tuesday, the kind that begins with the smell of weak coffee and the sound of a radiator clearing its throat. I was trying to explain to my twelve-year-old niece why the Moon always shows the same face to Earth. I had the diagrams—two oranges stabbed with a chopstick, one spinning, one stationary—and I had the patter: tidal locking, gravitational gradients, conservation of angular momentum. She nodded politely, but then asked, “If the Moon is locked, why does it wobble?” I opened my mouth, closed it, and felt the silence stretch like taffy. I didn’t know. I have two degrees in physics, and I didn’t know about lunar libration, not really. I’d heard the phrase, nodded sagely at it in colloquia, but the mechanical details lived in a fog.

That single moment cracked something open. Knowledge, I had told myself for decades, was a castle I was erecting, turret by turret, citation by citation. Instead it revealed itself as a shoreline, and every answer was also a question wearing a cheap disguise. The tide erodes as it delivers; the boundary moves twice a day. I had been so busy stockpiling sandbags that I never learned to swim.

So I began to court ignorance deliberately, the way other men buy motorcycles or take up salsa dancing at fifty. I scheduled “I don’t know” into my calendar, treated it like a standing coffee date. Every week I picked one thing everyone seems sure of—how planes fly, why the sky is blue, where electricity goes when it gets to ground—and followed the explanation one “why” deeper than comfort allowed. The exercise resembles peeling an onion, except each layer makes you weep in a new key.

Take the sky. Rayleigh scattering, sure: shorter wavelengths are more vigorously deflected by air molecules. But why are the molecules that size? Because electrons settle into orbital slots whose circumference must accommodate an integer number of de Broglie waves. And why do waves of matter oblige that integer rule? Because the wavefunction must be single-valued on rotation, lest the probability of finding two identical electrons add destructively and cancel to zero. Keep asking and soon you’re speaking in a mathematical tongue whose very nouns—group velocity, Lagrangian, Hilbert space—assume years of apprenticeship. Every student who has ever muttered “because math says so” stands at the base of a cliff whose summit is hidden by cloud, and no friendly porter appears to carry the bags. The blue sky, so blandly familiar, contains within it the entire history of quantum mechanics and therefore of twentieth-century genocide, computation, lasers, disco bar lights. A single postcard vista drags an invisible comet’s tail of human joy and atrocity. Knowing becomes less a stone in the hand and more a weather system you learn to read by the ache in an old injury.

I decided to keep going. I read textbooks the way retirees tour cathedrals: slowly, neck craned, occasionally sighing at the vaulted equations. I enrolled in a MOOC on number theory. Within three weeks I was drowning in proofs about primes that looked like knitting instructions for a Möbius scarf. The professor—an energetic Romanian who wore the same black turtleneck in every lecture—warned us that “mathematics is the study of trivial things.” He meant that each step is trivial once revealed, yet the chain of them becomes Himalaya. I kept pausing the video to stare out the window at actual Himalaya—my apartment faces a billboard for an airline, but in the smog the mountains can be anything—half expecting the skyline to rearrange itself into a commutative diagram.

Meanwhile life clattered on. The city’s infrastructure expressed its own unspoken theorems. One morning the subway lost its GPS clock; trains began arriving “now” according to no external standard. Commuters formed little temporal ponds, comparing the lag on their phones. A philosophical murmur rippled: What is time if nobody publishes it? I remembered Einstein’s remark that time is what clocks measure, and realized how politically naive that statement is. Time is what the Metropolitan Transit Authority is willing to testify to in court. A physicist’s definition presumes a cosmos; a rider’s presumes an authority. The same quantity—duration—careens between these poles like a drunk electron swapping orbitals.

During that week of schedule-free transit I found myself allocating attention the way gardeners ration water. Every unknown felt urgent. I read up on Byzantine fault tolerance, the algorithmic method by which computers vote to keep a common ledger even when some voters lie. It was invented to protect against rocket-ship failure but now keeps Bitcoin alive and, by extension, a small republic of miners in Inner Mongolia who measure wealth in exhaust fans. Knowledge that once lived in NASA white papers has seeped into the cracks of the world; you can find it swirling under a street vendor’s LED sign that flashes “LAMB OVER RICE $8.” The same mathematics that lands a rover on Mars is heating the planet to bake bread faster. Bread, rover—both are payloads we place on chemical fire, proof that the distance between sustenance and sublime is only scale.

All of which returns me to my niece. I invited her back, armed this time with cardboard, a lazy Susan, and the honesty of partial information. I explained that the Moon rocks slightly, nodding yes and shaking no, because its orbit is elliptical and tilted. Over 29 days we perceive a composite portrait assembled from tiny oscillations, like how a scanner builds a photo line by line. She asked why it bothers. I could have spoken of energy gradients, of how locking is a minimum-energy well, but instead I said, “Maybe celestial bodies negotiate how much of themselves to show, same as we do.” She seemed happier with that, probably because metaphor is the spoonful of sugar we use when the medicine tastes like footnote #27. I tucked her question into my pocket like a seed. Someday it may sprout into a paper on tidal dissipation in exoplanet oceans; more likely it will die in the dry soil of forgetfulness. Even that decay is chemistry worth knowing.

I began to suspect that ignorance pools in social layers as well as intellectual ones.
