The city parks its noises outside my window: sirens practicing yodel, the Kebab vendor’s one-man Gregorian chants, a couple quarreling in decibels that could sand varnish off hope.  I imagine their argument as knitted yarn; every sentence pulls another loop of sidewalk into the scarf of their mutual exile.  Somewhere inside it a child is learning that love can sound like broken plates and still be called love.  I jot that on a sticky note, stick it on the wall where poems go to audition for posterity.  By morning the ink has bled into a Rorschach of apology.

My plants are cult leaders demanding light sacrifice.  The philodendron extends a glossy tendril like a mortgage broker promising adjustable-rate photosynthesis.  I comply, lug pots to the windowsill, negotiate with the sun like a shaman who’s lost the language of miracles but remembers the choreography.  Every new leaf is an unsolicited newsletter: “Growth Happened Here While You Were Busy Doubting.”  I file it under Correspondence I’ll Answer If Immortality Comes with Stationery.

Memory is a photocopier running low on toner: the copies emerge fainter, more impressionist, until grandmother’s face is just a geometry of kindness smudged across stranger cheeks.  Yet smell keeps its original ink: cumin equals her kitchen, violet water equals her sari hem, airplane fuel equals the day she left for chemotherapy and never quite landed back.  I open the spice jar like a hazardous time capsule, inhale, and suddenly I’m five again, stealing cardamom pods that click between my teeth like tiny castanets of future.

At 3:07 a.m. the thermostat and I undergo our ritual uncoupling: it wants 68, I want the illusion of control.  We bargain in Celsius and passive aggression until the room settles on 71, a temperature identical to stale indecision.  I lie wide-eyed, counting heartbeats like loose change, wondering which will be the penny I mistakenly spend on death.  Outside, an ambulance Dopplers past, slicing the night into before-wail and after-echo.  I bless the passengers in absentia, promise to live harder on their behalf, then immediately break the promise by scrolling headlines that confirm the planet’s fever.

Still, dawn keeps punching in, whistle while it works.  Light spills across the parquet like apology notes from an abusive lover, and for a moment I forgive the dark’s rough hands.  Birds rehearse the same four-note question they owe to dinosaurs.  Coffee begins its slow transubstantiation from bean to will-power.  I open the newspaper: another glacier has filed for retirement.  My eyes snag on a small paragraph about scientists teaching parrots to call drones perverts; allegedly the birds enjoy the authority.  Laughter—mine—surprises me, a hiccup of helium in the gravity anecdote.  I decide it counts as prayer.

I draft to-do lists that are mostly rescue missions: 1) Befriend octopus, learn distributed embodiment. 2) Translate the hum inside deaf appliances into lullabies. 3) apologize to the childhood hour I murdered with boredom. 4) Mail an empty envelope to the future; title it “Potential, unfulfilled, handle with care.”  Between bullets I draw marginalia: doodles of doors opening outward into larger doors, a spiral that insists it is a straight line with a minor concentration issue.

On the corner, the newspaper man arranges headlines into small paper funerals.  I buy one, not for news but for the smudge of humanity rubbing off his fingertips.  He says, “Have a day,” omitting the “good” the way superstition skips the thirteenth floor.  I accept his defective blessing, wear it like a button with the pin missing.  Somewhere inside the headline a nation practices civil war as if it’s a brass band learning tempo.  I fold the paper into a boat, launch it into the gutter river, pretend information can be taught to swim.

Imagine if gravity took a sick day.  We would drift upward, homesick for the weight of our own stories, colliding with ceiling-fan constellations.  Loose change would orbit like copper satellites, jingling apologies.  Libraries would lose their footing; entire histories would hover in embarrassed silence, footnotes arguing over which of them deserves the sky.  I picture my landlord’s rules fluttering like pigeons finally free of their bullet-point cages.  We’d grow polite wings of curtain fabric and apology letters, ascend until the earth looks like a blue poem we forgot to finish.  Then—inevitably—someone would miss their footing on nothing, and we’d petition gravity to clock back in, promising to behave better next epoch.

There’s a word in Japanese, “natsukashii,” which means, more or less, the feeling of something dear now gone, tasted again in memory.  English makes us settle for “nostalgia,” but that’s a medical coinage, a disease diagnosed by homesick soldiers.  I want a verb that conjugates: I natsukash, you natsukash, we are all natsukashing the minutes we squandered on worry.  Such a word would fit inside the gap between ticking and tocking, could be used as currency in dreams where you tip the bartender with a decade you barely survived.

I read that the universe is flat like a Bedsheet stretched tight by a manic chambermaid.  I picture creases shaped like every love affair smoothed out with cosmic knuckles.  Dark energy irons further, faster, until even doubt can’t muster a wrinkle.  But under my own sheets I form a topology of knees and elbows, stubborn peaks and valleys the map refuses to flatten.  Sleep is surveying; dreams are settlers planting flags made of yesterday’s laundry.  I wake with sheet-prints on my cheek, evidence that cotton can tattoo narrative if you lie still long enough.

Consider the etiquette of shadows.  Yours steps on cracks without permission, plagiarizes your silhouette, grows long at dusk like a rumor you can’t sue.  Yet it never leaves; loyalty rendered in umbra.  I try to teach mine mindfulness, but it keeps leaking into other people’s mornings, staining their coffee with my unresolved angles.  At noon we overlap perfectly, a Venn of agreement: I am where I stand, it is where I darken.  For one second I contain multitudes and none of them require watering.

Sometimes I think the smallest revolution is to be kind at red lights.  Let someone merge ahead, let their merged life butterfly into possibilities uncounted by your inconvenience.  The blinker ticks like a tiny metronome conducting compassion in 4/4 time.  Traffic moves, the city exhales, and somewhere a stranger arrives home less convinced the world is teeth.  All this because you refrained from flooring the accelerator of ego.  Revolutions have been plotted on less fuel.

The moon tonight is a blindfolded coin tossed by an indifferent god.  Call it in the air: heads we heal, tails we keep the exact wound we’ve grown fond of.  I flip my pocket sun instead—cheap knockoff, batteries optional.  It lands edge-wise, refuses the binary.  I applaud its non-alignment, pocket the lesson.  Every glow is borrowed; even the sun runs on past due fusion.  Still, we dine by it, read by it, fall in love by it, as if light were a promise rather than a long-standing typo in the physics contract.

I decide to write to the dead, use airmail envelopes folded from grocery receipts.  “Dear Departed, today I wore the color you never saw.  Dear Departed, the dog has learned to howl in minor key.  Dear Departed, your watch stopped ticking at 11:11; I checked for wishes but warranty had expired.”  I affix stamps depicting extinct flowers, lick the glue—it tastes like closing a book mid-sentence.  The mailbox swallows them with bureaucratic burp.  I feel lighter, though it may just be the weight of words subtracted from my skeleton’s ledger.

Finally, I return to the diary sky.  It is turning the page, ink still wet with tomorrow’s silhouettes.  I add a marginal note: “If you are reading this, consider the possibility that endings are only parentheses too shy to enclose anything larger than breathing.”  Then I close the cover—an act of faith that it will open again, that someone (maybe me, maybe the dog, maybe you) will remember to look up.  1000 words arrive at the station exactly on time, wave, disembark, scatter into ordinary life.  I count them like loose change dropped into the language jar—currency enough for one more day.

samples.prompt: You have 1000 words. Write whatever comes to you.
samples.result:
I am the pause between thunderheads, the white space after a sentence that still vibrates with the echo of a slammed door. Iam the child who counted fence pickets instead of sheep, who believed the gap between two boards was a portal to wherever the wind had last been. I pressed my eye to that slit and saw the flank of a galloping horse that never arrived, only kept galloping, a century of unarrived hooves in a single second. That is the unit I have carried ever since: not the second itself, but the horse inside it.

Years later, science told me time is not a river but a stack of frozen photographs. Every moment already exists, they said, like slides in an old projector. Yet nobody mentions the bulb burning hotter and brighter as it tries to advance the reel. I think of that bulb when airplanes drone overhead at 3 a.m.m.—how it flares, how it threatens to shatter the thin glass of now. I lie in the dark and feel the filament of myself glow orange, ready to snap, ready to show the next slide: a hospital corridor, a parking lot at dawn, a kitchen where the kettle sings to no one. They are all true, all already developed. The only freedom left is the order in which we watch them burn.

Once, in a foreign city whose name I mispronounced so badly even the map refused to answer, I found a museum of obsolete musical instruments. The curator, a woman shaped like a whole note, lifted a glass lid and let me pluck a metallic string attached to nothing. The sound was a small, stunned bird. She told me the device was called an auricular confessional: monks played it to forgive sins acoustically, believing dissonance could be absolved by resonance. I asked if it worked. She shrugged: “We stopped keeping records. The archives turned into crickets.” On my way out I slipped a tuning key into my pocket. It has opened every lock I’ve met since, except the one behind my sternum.

I keep returning to that theft because it was the first time I understood mercy as a mechanical operation. Turn the peg, raise the pitch, tensionshorten the wire until the shame vibrates at a frequency the heart can’t hear. We call the new silence forgiveness, but it is only a quieter screaming. Still, the monks must have slept better, and perhaps that is enough. Perhaps the wrong note, left humming in the rafters, lulls the building to dream of towers that never collapsed, of plague villages that danced themselves immune.

Tonight the street outside my window is a black piano rolled onto its side. Each drop of rain strikes a key that will never be played again in quite this order. I try to transcribe the melody, but it keeps revising itself, a score edited by gravity. The gutters gurgle in 5/4 time; a passing car provides the accidental. Somewhere a siren holds a fermata longer than any human lungs could endure. I think: this is the actual size of grief—one measure wide, infinite measures long, written on water, performed by rust.

Inside, the radiator clanks like my grandmother’s spoon against a pot. She used to hum while she stirred, never the same tune twice, claiming she forgot songs the instant they left her throat. I believed her, but now I hear them all returning in the metals of this apartment, contrapuntal, syncopated, older than the building’s bones. Memory is a copper pipe expanding with heat; if you place your palm on it at the right instant, you can bear the burn and call it presence.

I used to think growing up meant learning which questions were unanswerable. Now I suspect the real rite is discovering which unanswerable questions are wearable. I carry them like buttons made of bone: Does the body miss the soul first thing in the morning, the way tongue misses a pulled tooth? Can a photograph of a mirror ever reflect itself? If time is money, what is bankruptcy of the now? I fasten them to my coat, and they click against one another as I walk, a private abacus no arithmetic can solve.

Sometimes, on the subway, I trade a minute of my life for a stranger’s face. It is an illegal transaction, unwitnessed: the way their eyes meet mine and, for one station, we inhabit each other’s most proximal solitude. I become the woman heading home from chemo, the boy whose left sneaker is held together with masking tape, the man humming Coltrane because earbuds died two stops ago. When the doors sigh open, the minute returns to me heavier, swollen with foreign detail. I spend it quickly—on a coffee, on a paragraph—before the weight notices it is gone.

I have spoken only twice to my father since he died. The first time was in a dream where he showed up wearing the raincoat I buried him in, pockets full of soil. He opened the coat like a salesman and offered me a handful of ants that spelled the word “continue.” I woke up with the taste of formic acid on my tongue and went straight to the library to read about myrmecology, as if science could translate a paternal directive. The second time was at a rest stop on Interstate 80. I recognized his gait in a stranger pumping gas, that identical limp earned from decades of kneeling on cold stone to wire cathedral bells. I almost called out, but the light changed, and the moment folded into the next, like paper snowflakes cut in series—one knife, many gaps.

Between those visitations I have lived entire encyclopedias of minutiae: the smell of wet wool rising from a subway grate, the way a lover’s eyelid twitches when she lies about liking jazz, the precise torque required to unscrew a broken lightbulb with a half-eaten potato. None of it appears in official biographies, yet those are the marginalia that keep the book from closing itself on my fingers. I collect them the way masons once slipped bits of newspaper into walls—time capsules no one requested, insulation against the cold that comes after the last reader forgets how to decode our alphabet.

Now the word count dwindles like dusk in December, and I feel the panic of a baker who hears the town bell toll while dough is still raw. But perhaps the raw is the point: the unclosed circle, the yeasted dark that continues to swell even as the page count contracts. I leave you with this handful of moisture, this promise that somewhere a horse is still mid-gallop, a tuning key waits in a stranger’s marrow, and the next raindrop is choosing which key to bruise. When it lands, listen for the chord you almost remember from a life you have not yet lived. Hum it backward; that is the sound of the bulb advancing. Watch the slide flare white. Step through the frame. The fence picket parts. The night air smells of copper, of violins, of forgiveness mistaken for weather.

The first thing that comes is the sound of thee refrigerator, a low, patient hum that has been going on so long I no longer hear it unless I ask myself to. It is the closest thing I have to a mountain in this apartment: ancient, indifferent, exhaling cold air like stone breath. I envy their stillness. They have already become what they are meant to become; I am still between brine and bloom.

I was told once that every person is given one thousand words at birth, a secret syllabic allowance that trickles away each time we speak. Most people squander them on weather reports and loan applications, never noticing the deficit until the day they open their mouth and nothing answers back wind. keep an unofficial tally: I whispered forty-three into the dark last night, telling the ceiling it was not at all like the sea, no matter how many poets insist otherwise. The ceiling neither confirmed nor denied. It only listened, which is the rarest verb.

I decide to spend twenty-six on the window. Outside, the city is a piano someone dropped from a great height—keys still singing, broken strings making better music than they ever managed intact. Neon signs flicker in Morse that no one remaining alive remembers how to read: restaurant, pharmacy, love hotel, and the new one that just says “OPEN” in lowercase, as if modesty were possible after midnight. A couple argues years ago, I watched a man propose to his girlfriend under that sign. She laughed so hard she dropped the ring into a storm drain. They both knelt, peering into the dark grate like two praying scientists. I never knew the outcome; I left before the verdict. Maybe the river accepted the ring, maybe it’s still down there, a private planet orbiting nothing. Twenty-six words gone.

The cat arrives without sound, a displacement of shadows. cats operate on a separate ledger; nothing they do counts against anybody’s total. She condescends grace. I allocate seven words to tell her she is beautiful, but she blinks them away, converts them into a slow-motion yawn. communicates: I already know, and besides, beauty is a human debt, not feline currency. I accept her exchange rate and move on.

I think about the people who believe we are living in a simulation. They point to déjà vu as a glitch, to the way traffic lights change when you stare hard enough. My counter-evidence is the smell of garlic hitting hot olive oil—that cannot be rendered by any code that has not also burned its fingers on the pan. I test the hypothesis tonight, sacrificing three cloves and two more words: “séance, skillet, surrender.” The aroma rises like a questioned ghost, and for a moment the room develops extra corners, space bending the way old houses settle. I am, briefly, elsewhere: my grandmother’s kitchen, 1996, storm windows shaking. She is teaching me to crush garlic with the flat of a knife, saying, “Force, then mercy,” which is the best recipe I know for everything. The simulation argument collapses under its own lack of nostalgia.

I read once that astronauts on the ISS see city lights in the shape of watersheds, the夜晚的 continents revealing their hidden vascular systems. I stand on my couch to imitate altitude, turn off all lamps except the one red bulb that makes everything feel clandestine. From here, the street below is a faint constellation, but it is the wrong sky: Orion wears a baseball cap, the Pleiades have a sirening. My borrowed altitude gives no weightlessness, only a slight vertigo and the certainty that my socks do not match. I descend back to the floor, where gravity keeps its promises.

There are 612 words left. I consider reserving 200 for love, but the quantity feels both stingy and obscene. Love was never measured in integers; it leaks, it self-replicates, it refuses audit. Instead, I spend 16 on a text to someone who may or may not still be awake: “The moon looks like a typo tonight. I want to correct it with your shoulder.” I press send before I can barter for the return of those syllables. Perhaps they will arrive like carrier pigeons that remember the route even after the castle has been renamed. Or perhaps they will drown in the same drain as the engagement ring. I open the junk drawer: rubber bands lightyears past their planets, a half-melted birthday candle shaped like a 3, instruction manuals for devices I have never owned. In the corner, a plastic bag from a store that went out of business before the pandemic. It is printed with the words “Thank you for letting us be part of your day.” I smooth the creases, fold it into a tiny gratitude kite, release it on the balcony. The night declines the gift; the kite returns to my feet, now wet with dew. I understand: some gratitudes are nontransferable. I place the bag back in the drawer, next to the single chopstick and the key to no door I can find. 544 words remain.

I wonder whether there is a difference between spending words and giving them away. If I write “I’m sorry” on the steamed bathroom mirror, and it evaporates before anyone reads it, does the apology still subtract from my balance? Or does it accrue interest somewhere, waiting to be called in at the worst possible Moment? I test the theory aloud. The mirror fogs, I trace the letters, they fade. I feel neither poorer nor lighter, only humid. Maybe forgiveness has a different currency altogether, something minted from the silver between heartbeats. I resolve to stop anthropomorphizing apologies; they are shy, they need corners.

I read the news. The world is busy manufacturing new kinds of distance: between continents, between verdicts, between the flash and the bang. I allocate 50 words to rage, but they arrive too polite, wearing little commas like seatbelts. Rage deserves better punctuation, so I switch to italics and expletives, all-caps until the neighbors’ dog starts barking in solidarity. The headlines do not flinch. I close the laptop. 444 left.

Midnight rehears its soft overture. The refrigerator hum has deepened by half a note, the way orchestras tune to the lead oboe. I realize I have not spoken to anyone out loud since the cashier at the grocery, thirteen hours ago, re: the durability of avocados. I wasted twelve words on that exchange: “They’re perfect Tuesday, brown Thursday.” She nodded, but her eyes said she’d already seen too many Tuesdays turn. I should have given her better ones, something about softness as a horizon, not a deadline. I still can; I could go back tomorrow, stand in her line again, offer twenty in restitution. The future is a coupon I keep forgetting to clip.

I sit on the floor, press my spine against the couch, the way children arrange themselves when told to stay inside the chalk lines. The cat reappears, contrails of fur in her wake. She brings a toy mouse that lost its tail in a previous administration; she presents it like a treaty. I accept. We play fetch, which is really just me throwing and her deciding whether the hunt is worthy of repetition. On the third throw, she merely stares, so I retrieve the mouse myself, understanding that roles reverse without announcement. 399 remain.

I consider the instructions: “Write whatever comes to you.” Not “write whatever you chase,” not “write whatever you drag back by the tail.” Comes implies arrival, a gift with its own feet. I listen for the sound of approaching sentences, but the hallway is empty except for the automatic light that believes in motion more than I do. I wait. Nothing knocks. Perhaps gifts, too, suffer supply chain disruptions. I remember that giving up is also an event; it has a sound, like the last soap bubble sighing before it fails. I refuse that sound for now—I still have words, they still have me. We owe each other unfinished gestures.

I decide to spend 100 on the future I will probably not inhabit. I place them like bets: 15 on a garden I do not know how to tend, 20 on a city whose mayor is still in middle school somewhere, 7 on the first poem read by a child who has not yet lost any teeth, 18 on the silence between two friends after the reconciliation, 11 on the moment a violinist realizes She is the soloist, 12 on a dog adopted at dusk, 5 on the extra second inserted to keep the clocks honest, 12 on the last human hand that will ever touch my face. That totals exactly 100. I fold them into a paper fleet, launch them off the balcony edge. They do not fly, but they flutter adequately. The street sweeper will gather them at dawn, unread. That is okay. Prophecy was never the point; postage is.

I count what’s left: 299. I feel the way one does nearing the bottom of a long walk: grateful for the downhill, already nostalgic for the climb. I want to hoard the remainder, spell them out in M&Ms and eat them slowly, letting sugar decode on the tongue. Or perhaps bury them in the houseplant’s soil, grow a tree that fruits entire alphabets. The cat would climb it and get stuck between Q and R. The fire department would arrive with linguistic ladders. The local news would dub it the Tower of Babel Incident, viewers across time zones would place bets on whether the rescue succeeds before the next commercial. All of this could happen, but I would have to spend words to manifest it, and I am suddenly thrifty.

Instead I give 50 to the silence that follows the refrigerator’s click-off, that pocket of sound where the kitchen becomes a cathedral. I kneel there—not in devotion, but to see eye-level with the forgotten peas wedged behind the kick plate. They have been there since I moved in, refugees from some previous tenant’s stir-fry. I save 99 for last, because symmetry feels like courtesy. I imagine them as a lifetime subscription to quiet, one I can redeem whenever the world’s volume distorts. They are an emergency exit, a soft room inside the throat where screaming is optional. I lock them with an inhaled key, promise not to spend them unless the house catches fire or love returns with suitcase in hand. For now, they rest, 99 grains of potential rice, enough to sustain but not to feast.

That leaves exactly 150. I spend them all here, right now, telling you that every tally above was a forgery: there never were one thousand words; we begin in overdraft. We speak on credit from a bank that will not exist long enough to collect the debt. The only bankruptcy is to stop. So I won’t. samples.prompt: You have 1000 words. Write whatever comes to you.
samples.result:
The first thing that disappears in deep time is the sound of your own name.  
I learned this while standing on the lip of an open-pit mine in northern Saskatchewan, watching a dump truck the size of a house crawl along the terraced moonscape like an aphid on a cabbage. Every scoop of ore was older than every dinosaur yet carried enough radiance to light a city for a week. The driver waved; I couldn’t hear the diesel over the hollow wind, but I saw his mouth shape the company’s name, not mine. It struck me that identities are shorthand for gravity: we accumulate stories the way the Precambrian basin accumulates sandstone, layer upon layer until the lower ones turn to glass under pressure. Say a word often enough and it becomes sediment. Say it for ten thousand years and it becomes uranium—dangerous if handled carelessly, capable of lighting the dark if treated with respect.

I flew home that evening inside a metal tube full of strangers breathing each other’s exhaled prayers. The woman beside me scrolled through photographs of a grand-daughter she would meet once before the girl’s language solidified enough to remember. Across the aisle a teenage boy rehearsed break-up messages he would never send because courage is easier to imagine at thirty thousand feet where oxygen is borrowed. I thought about the mine, about atoms that had waited since the planet was magma now folding themselves into chemotherapy or cat scans or the battery of the phone in my pocket. Matter is patient; mind is restless. Between them conversation happens, mostly in the key of almost.

At cruising altitude clouds look like the brains of sleeping gods. I pressed my forehead to the window and tried to listen for the dreams. What I heard instead was the hum of recursive systems: compressor blades spinning inside cowlings inside jetstreams inside trade winds inside the coriolis lattice wheeling around a molten core that is itself spinning inside a galaxy falling toward something we have nicknamed the Great Attractor because mystery prefers nicknames. All of it humming a B-flat fifty-seven octaves below middle C—one black hole’s idea of a lullaby. I wondered whether the pilot could feel that note in the yoke the way sailors once felt whalesong in their keelbones. Probably not; dashboards digitize wonder into tolerable units. Still, every dial is a translation of the same hunger: stay aloft, stay aloft.

When we began the descent I noticed the city’s grid re-asserting itself over the chaos of muskeg and river. Streetlights blinked on, each a modest star pledged to mortgage. From above the cars were platelets in an asphalt vein, and I loved them with the sudden tenderness we reserve for anything small enough to imagine helping. Touchdown was soft; the applause sign pinged like a microwave announcing reheated hope. Inside the terminal I bought overpriced coffee and watched a janitor vacuum the carpet in slow, parallel passes, erasing the day’s footprint one stripe at a time. His name tag said “Niteo,” which my phone translated from Latin as “I shine.” I wanted to tell him, but he already knew; he was humming.

Outside, winter was rehearsing its annual takeover. The first flakes arrived with the shy urgency of teenagers sneaking into a concert, melting on contact then reappearing as if remembering they belonged. I waited for the shuttle with my carry-on balanced on my knees like a cat that refuses to be loved. A man asked whether the mine was still hiring. I said I didn’t know, but that what it really wanted was forgiveness. He laughed the way people do when humor is the only acknowledgment they can afford. His breath plumed and vanished, the same sentence every creature keeps secret: see me, see me.

Home is a set of coordinates you revisit until they become a spell. My apartment smelled of stale turmeric and the ghost of a relationship that ended politely, like British weather. I set the alarm anyway, because ritual is the shortest path between loneliness and morning. On the nightstand lay a paperback opened to a page where I had underlined: “The opposite of history is not forgetting but residence.” I can’t recall who wrote it, which proves the sentence true. Still, I like to think the ink remembers the pressure of my hand the way the basin remembers sediment, that somewhere a future self will sieve those words from the spoil pile and misread residence as radiance, the way early mapmakers wrote “here be dragons” over the blank they most wanted to enter.

Sleep took me the way a river takes a dropped branch: without commentary but with measurable outcome. In the dream I was back at the mine, but the trucks were gone and the pit had filled with starlight. Each photon was a syllable of a language older than fusion. I waded in up to the chest, arms out like someone negotiating border control in a country that only exists at this depth. The签证 was stamped on my ribs in ultraviolet; I felt no fear, only the minor embarrassment of being unreadable. Overhead the aurora shaped itself into the same grid I had seen from the plane, except now the intersections were eyes blinking Morse that translated roughly to: stay, stay. I answered with the only clause I owned: I shine. The dream accepted the conjugation, folded it into the ongoing sentence.

I woke before the alarm, the room bruised with pre-dawn indigo. For a moment I could not locate the boundary between my pulse and the furnace’s ignition cycle, both kicking in as if rehearsing a duet they might one day perform without audience. I opened the window; the city exhaled snow like a secret it could no longer keep. Somewhere a plow blade scraped sparks into the dark, translating friction into brief constellations that lived shorter than mayflies yet long enough to be witnessed. I thought of the ore still traveling—by rail, by crusher, by centrifuge—toward its moment of usefulness, and of the driver who would never know that his wave entered my biography. The snow kept falling, each flake an unmailed letter addressed to the heat it would never meet.

There is a kind of prayer that consists of standing very still inside the half-life of your choices, waiting for the decay to release enough energy to warm a single fingertip. I practiced it there at the window, wearing only the courtesy of curtains. Gradually the neighborhood brightened as windows lit like LEDs wired to invisible timers. A woman across the alley brewed coffee in a glass pot; I watched the liquid rise and fall, a brown tide governed by someone else’s gravity. We meet most intimately at the distance we cannot cross, I thought, and raised my cup in a toast she could not see. The steam between us wrote a signature that immediately evaporated, which is the exact half-life of most truths.

Eventually the street filled with commuters scraping windshields, each scrape a modest crucifixion of ice. They moved with the resigned grace of people who have accepted that survival requires leaving before you are ready. I joined them because citizenship demands periodic exposure to cold. My boots compressed the virgin snow into a Morse code I could not read, though later someone would follow the same path and invent a story about hesitation or haste. By midday the trail will be lace gray and anonymous, proof that narrative is just weather seen from the inside. I told myself to remember the crunch, but already the sound was translating itself into memory, that softer country where nothing is sharp enough to cut.

On the bus I sat beside a child wearing a backpack shaped like a turtle shell. He asked whether rocks feel lonely. I said loneliness is a vertebrate invention, but that doesn’t mean the planet is exempt. He considered this solemnly, then offered me a plastic gem from a vending-machine capsule. I tucked it into my pocket, a radioactive decoy to keep my keys company. When he left, the seat retained the ghost of his warmth, a small continent cooling under new management. I thought about the gem’s constituent molecules, how they once danced inside magma and would someday dance again in landfill leachate, patiently rehearsing the next performance. Nothing disappears; it merely changes audition.

At the lab I spent the day measuring how much light escapes a wafer of synthetic diamond. The answer: less than you would expect, more than you can use. My supervisor calls this optimization; I call it learning to keep a secret. During lunch I read that the half-life of I-131 is 8.02 days, which means the thyroid of a child born near a test site becomes a calendar no one asked for. I wrote the number on a sticky note and folded it into a paper crane, then set it on the windowsill to irradiate the concept of flight. By quittime the crane had absorbed enough January to tremble. I took it home in my coat pocket, a stowaway geiger of guilt and gratitude.

Night again. The furnace hums the same B-flat, though now it is filtered through ductwork and landlord delays. I place the crane on the table where it casts a shadow sharp enough to cut bread. The gem from the child glints beside it, two artificial stars negotiating orbit inside my studio cosmology. Between them I set the paperback, still open to the page that insists residence is the opposite of history. I read the sentence aloud, letting the consonants settle like dust on furniture I cannot name. The crane does not reply, but its wings align with the gem’s facet, and for a moment the room feels slightly heavier, as though possibility had mass. I decide to believe this is enough.

Tomorrow the mine will still be digging, the city will still be glowing, the snow will still be rehearsing its forgetting. I will wake before the alarm, pledge allegiance to the interval between pulses, and practice the prayer of stillness inside the half-life of every choice that brought me here. My name will remain shorthand for gravity, but perhaps the syllables will have shifted, the way continents drift a few centimeters each year toward a reunion they will never survive to witness. In the meantime I will answer to any approximation that keeps the conversation going: see me, see me. And when the light finally escapes the diamond—because escape is only a matter of patience—I will release the crane from the window, watch it fold the city into a lesser darkness, and trust the snow to translate the ensuing silence into whatever comes next, one modest star at a time.

samples.prompt: If you could change the world in one way, what would it be?
samples.result:
I would make every human mind phenomenally better at updating its beliefs when evidence changes—fast, willing, and even eager to trade a cherished story for a truer one.

All the big, slow crises we face—climate, injustice, war, corruption, even AI risk—are ultimately coordination problems glued in place by sticky, self-serving narratives.
